summary: This PR introduces a `should_strip_auth` helper in `sessions.py` to decide whether the `Authorization` header should be removed when following redirects, replacing inline logic in `rebuild_auth`. The logic correctly handles hostname changes, scheme changes, and the http→https upgrade exemption. The main outstanding issues are a port normalization bug that conflates explicit default ports with implicit ones, several test coverage gaps (especially around pure port-change redirects and the explicit-standard-port combination), and a handful of minor style nits. (1 finding(s) below the 0.4 confidence threshold hidden.)

findings.title: Port comparison treats explicit default port as different from implicit default port
findings.detail:
The final `return` on line 132 compares `old_parsed.port != new_parsed.port` using raw `urlparse` values, where `.port` is `None` when no port is specified. This means a redirect from `http://example.com:80/foo` to `http://example.com/bar` returns `True` (strip auth), even though port 80 is HTTP's default and the two URLs refer to the same server. Similarly, `https://example.com:443/foo` → `https://example.com/bar` would incorrectly strip auth.

Note: the https→http downgrade cases (f5) were analyzed and found to be correctly handled — the scheme check on the same line catches all relevant cases. The pure same-scheme explicit-vs-implicit default-port mismatch (e.g. `http://example.com:80` → `http://example.com`) is the concrete remaining bug.

Fix: normalize ports before comparison by resolving `None` to the scheme's default (80 for http, 443 for https), or compare after normalizing the netloc.

findings.title: test_should_strip_auth_port_change changes scheme AND port — no pure port-change test exists
findings.detail: The test `test_should_strip_auth_port_change` uses `http://example.com:1234/foo` → `https://example.com:4321/bar`, which changes both scheme and port simultaneously. This makes the test name misleading and leaves the `old_parsed.port != new_parsed.port` branch on line 132 untested in isolation (same-scheme, different-port, e.g. `http://example.com:1234` → `http://example.com:5678`). Either rename the test to reflect that it covers a combined scheme+port change, and add a new test for the pure port-change case (same scheme, different port), so the relevant branch of line 132 is exercised independently.

findings.title: No integration test for auth stripping on http→https redirect with non-standard ports
findings.detail: Unit tests for `should_strip_auth` cover non-standard-port cases, but there is no integration/end-to-end test that performs an actual http→https redirect with a non-standard port and verifies the `Authorization` header is physically absent in the forwarded request via `rebuild_auth`. Unit coverage of the helper alone does not guarantee the wiring in `rebuild_auth` works correctly for this path.

findings.title: No test for http→https upgrade with both explicit standard ports (http:80 → https:443)
findings.detail: The special-case branch at lines 128-130 permits auth forwarding for http (port 80 or None) → https (port 443 or None). Existing tests check one explicit port at a time, but never the combination `http://example.com:80/foo` → `https://example.com:443/bar` where both sides use explicit standard ports. This combination path through the branch is not exercised.

findings.title: No guard against relative or malformed URLs reaching should_strip_auth
findings.detail: If `old_url` or `new_url` is a relative or opaque URI, `urlparse().hostname` returns `None` for both. The hostname check on line 122 then passes (no strip triggered), and the subsequent scheme/port checks may also fail to trigger, potentially forwarding auth to an unintended destination. While `resolve_redirects` is expected to always provide fully-qualified absolute URLs, there is no assertion or guard inside `should_strip_auth` itself to enforce this precondition. Consider adding a guard (e.g. early return `True` if either hostname is `None`) or at minimum a docstring note about the precondition.

findings.title: No test for rebuild_auth short-circuit when Authorization header is absent
findings.detail: `rebuild_auth` only calls `should_strip_auth` when an `Authorization` header is present. There is no test verifying that `rebuild_auth` does not error and does not call `should_strip_auth` when no `Authorization` header exists and a redirect crosses scheme/host, exercising the short-circuit path.

findings.title: No test for should_strip_auth when one hostname is None (malformed URL edge case)
findings.detail: There is no test where one URL has a missing or `None` hostname (e.g. a relative URL or malformed URL), so the behavior of `should_strip_auth` in this edge case is untested. This is related to the guard concern in f1.

findings.title: Docstring missing trailing period
findings.detail: The docstring `"""Decide whether Authorization header should be removed when redirecting"""` has no trailing period, inconsistent with other method docstrings in the file (e.g. `"""Receives a Response. Returns a generator of Responses or Requests."""`). Add a period for consistency.

findings.title: Tests instantiate full Session rather than SessionRedirectMixin
findings.detail: The new `should_strip_auth` unit tests create a full `requests.Session()` to test a single mixin method. Since `SessionRedirectMixin` is already imported (line 29), using it directly would be lighter and more consistent with the intent of testing the mixin's behavior in isolation.

findings.title: http→https exemption logic is correct but asymmetry is worth documenting
findings.detail: The special-case block at lines 128-130 exempts auth stripping only for http→https upgrades on standard ports, not for https→http downgrades (which always strip via the scheme check on line 132). This asymmetry is intentional and correct from a security standpoint. A brief comment explaining the asymmetry would help future maintainers avoid accidentally 'fixing' it.

findings.title: Variable naming inconsistent with pre-existing conventions in the file
findings.detail: The new code uses `old_parsed` / `new_parsed` while the surrounding pre-existing code used `original_parsed` / `redirect_parsed`. The new names are arguably cleaner, but the inconsistency is worth noting. Consider aligning with the existing convention or updating all usages for consistency.

findings.title: IDN / Unicode hostname normalization not performed before comparison
findings.detail: `urlparse().hostname` lowercases hostnames but does not perform IDNA normalization. Two hostnames that are IDNA-equivalent (e.g. a Unicode label vs its punycode form) will compare as unequal strings, causing unnecessary auth stripping. The reverse direction (homoglyph attack) correctly causes stripping, so there is no security regression — only a minor correctness/UX issue for users with internationalized hostnames. Worth documenting as a known limitation.

findings.title: Port comparison treats explicit default port as different from implicit default port
findings.detail:
The final `return` on line 132 compares `old_parsed.port != new_parsed.port` using the raw `urlparse` values, where `.port` is `None` when no port is specified in the URL. This means `http://example.com:80/foo` → `http://example.com/bar` returns `True` (strip auth), even though port 80 is the default for HTTP and the two URLs refer to the same server. Similarly, `https://example.com:443/foo` → `https://example.com/bar` would incorrectly strip auth.

The special-case http→https block (lines 128-130) already handles `old_parsed.port in (80, None)` and `new_parsed.port in (443, None)`, but the general same-scheme case at line 132 does no such normalization.

Fix: normalize the port before comparison, e.g. resolve `None` to the scheme's default port (80 for http, 443 for https) before comparing, or use a helper like `netloc` comparison after normalizing default ports.

findings.title: Case-sensitive hostname comparison may allow auth leakage across equivalent hostnames
findings.detail: urlparse().hostname lowercases the hostname (RFC 3986 §3.2.2 says hostnames are case-insensitive), so the comparison on line 122 (`old_parsed.hostname != new_parsed.hostname`) should be safe in practice. However, the comparison on line 132 uses `.scheme` via urlparse which is also lowercased, so that part is fine too. BUT there is a subtlety: if either URL is malformed or has a non-ASCII IDN hostname, urlparse may return surprising results. More concretely: if `old_url` or `new_url` has no hostname (e.g. it is a relative URL or opaque URI that somehow reaches this function), `old_parsed.hostname` returns `None` for both and the hostname check on line 122 passes (no strip), then the scheme check on line 132 may also not trigger, causing auth to be forwarded to an unexpected destination. The function has no guard against malformed or relative URLs. Verify that `resolve_redirects` always produces a fully-qualified absolute URL before calling `rebuild_auth`/`should_strip_auth`.

findings.title: http→https upgrade exception does not guard against non-standard source ports, enabling auth forwarding from a non-standard http port to https
findings.detail: The special-case exemption at lines 128-130 allows auth to be forwarded only when `old_parsed.port in (80, None)` AND `new_parsed.port in (443, None)`. This looks correct and the tests at line 1607-1608 in test_requests.py confirm it. However, consider the reverse port-normalisation issue: a URL written as `http://example.com:80/foo` has `old_parsed.port == 80`, so the exception fires and returns `False` (no strip). But a URL written as `https://example.com:443/bar` also has `new_parsed.port == 443`, so the exception fires too. These are all handled. The real gap is that the check only covers *this specific* http→https upgrade path. An http→https upgrade where the destination is a non-standard port (e.g. 8443) correctly strips auth (line 132 catches it). This logic appears correct, but the asymmetry — where http→https on standard ports is exempted but https→http is never exempted — is intentional and correct for the security fix. No action needed beyond confirming the tests cover the port edge cases (they do).

findings.title: No stripping of auth on redirect to a URL-encoded / percent-encoded variant of a different hostname
findings.detail: urlparse().hostname does not perform IDNA normalisation or Unicode normalisation. An attacker-controlled redirect server could respond with a `Location:` header whose hostname is a Unicode homoglyph or an IDNA-equivalent of a different host (e.g. `xn--` encoded variant vs plain ASCII). urlparse will compare these as unequal strings even if they resolve to the same address, which would incorrectly strip auth. Conversely, two superficially different Unicode hostnames that IDNA-resolve to the same punycode label would be compared as different strings, stripping auth unnecessarily (a correctness issue, not a security issue). The security concern is the homoglyph direction, where `example.com` and `еxample.com` (Cyrillic е) compare as unequal — this correctly causes stripping, so the security outcome is safe. This is a low-severity note for awareness.

findings.title: Proxy-Authorization header is not considered in should_strip_auth / rebuild_auth
findings.detail: The `rebuild_auth` method correctly strips the `Authorization` header when crossing root URLs. However, a caller could set a `Proxy-Authorization` header directly on the request (not via proxy configuration). The `rebuild_proxies` method at line 295 unconditionally deletes `Proxy-Authorization` before re-adding it from the proxy config, so this is handled for proxy auth. But if a user manually set `Proxy-Authorization` without going through the proxy config, it will be deleted even on same-host redirects. This is existing behavior unchanged by this PR; noting for awareness but not a regression introduced here.

findings.title: https→http downgrade with explicit port 80 on destination is NOT stripped — auth leaks in plaintext
findings.detail:
Consider the redirect: `https://example.com/foo` → `http://example.com:80/bar`. Walk through the logic:
1. `old_parsed.hostname == new_parsed.hostname` → both `example.com`, so line 122 does NOT return True (no strip from hostname check).
2. The special-case at line 128 checks `old_parsed.scheme == 'http'` → False (old is https), so the block is skipped.
3. Line 132: `old_parsed.port != new_parsed.port` → `None != 80` → **True**, so it returns True (strip). ✓

Now consider: `https://example.com:443/foo` → `http://example.com/bar`.
1. Hostname same → no strip from line 122.
2. Special-case: `old_parsed.scheme == 'http'` → False, skipped.
3. Line 132: `old_parsed.port != new_parsed.port` → `443 != None` → **True**, strip. ✓

Now consider: `https://example.com/foo` → `http://example.com/bar` (the core https→http downgrade).
1. Hostname same.
2. Special-case: old scheme is 'https', not 'http' → skipped.
3. Line 132: `old_parsed.port != new_parsed.port` → `None != None` → False. `old_parsed.scheme != new_parsed.scheme` → `'https' != 'http'` → **True** → returns True (strip). ✓

All https→http cases appear correctly handled. Retracting this as critical — the logic is sound. Setting to nit to document that the analysis was done.

findings.title: Docstring missing period at end of sentence
findings.detail: The docstring `"""Decide whether Authorization header should be removed when redirecting"""` has no trailing period. All other method docstrings in the file end with a period (e.g. `"""Receives a Response. Returns a generator of Responses or Requests."""`). Add a period for consistency.

findings.title: Intermediate variables `old_parsed`/`new_parsed` use slightly inconsistent naming versus previous local variables
findings.detail: The removed code used `original_parsed` / `redirect_parsed` as its local names. While `old_parsed` / `new_parsed` is arguably clearer, within the rest of the file the pattern is `original_parsed` / `redirect_parsed`. The new names are fine on their own, but it is a minor inconsistency with the nearby conventions that existed just lines away. Low-priority, but worth noting.

findings.title: Unit tests for `should_strip_auth` instantiate a full `Session` rather than `SessionRedirectMixin`
findings.detail: The existing style sample already imports `SessionRedirectMixin` (line 29) and the surrounding tests that exercise mixin-level behaviour tend to operate at the mixin level. Creating a full `requests.Session()` just to test a single mixin method is slightly heavier than necessary and inconsistent with that import. Consider using `SessionRedirectMixin()` directly, or at minimum document why a full Session is used.

findings.title: `test_should_strip_auth_port_change` changes scheme as well as port, making the test name misleading
findings.detail: The test is named `test_should_strip_auth_port_change` but the URLs used (`http://…:1234` → `https://…:4321`) change both the scheme AND the port. A pure port-change test would keep the scheme the same (e.g. `http://…:1234` → `http://…:4321`). Either rename the test or adjust the URLs so the name accurately describes what is being exercised.

findings.title: No test for http→https upgrade with explicit standard ports on both sides simultaneously
findings.detail: The special-case branch at lines 128-130 allows http (port 80 or None) → https (port 443 or None). The existing tests check each side individually (http:80→https, http→https:443) but never test the combination http:80→https:443 (both explicit standard ports). This is a minor gap but the branch combination is not fully exercised.

findings.title: No test for same-scheme, same-host, port change without scheme change (pure port-change redirect)
findings.detail: test_should_strip_auth_port_change tests 'http://example.com:1234/foo' → 'https://example.com:4321/bar', which is both a port AND scheme change. There is no test covering same-scheme port changes, e.g. http://example.com:1234 → http://example.com:5678, which exercises only the `old_parsed.port != new_parsed.port` part of line 132.

findings.title: No test for should_strip_auth when Authorization header is absent (rebuild_auth path)
findings.detail: The refactored rebuild_auth (line 255) only calls should_strip_auth when 'Authorization' is present. There is no test that verifies rebuild_auth does NOT call should_strip_auth (i.e., does not error) when Authorization is absent and a redirect crosses scheme/host, exercising the short-circuit behaviour.

findings.title: No test for should_strip_auth when hostnames are identical but one is None
findings.detail: The hostname comparison on line 122-123 returns True if hostnames differ. There is no test where one URL has a missing/None hostname (e.g. a relative URL or malformed URL), verifying the behaviour in edge cases that could surface via unusual redirect targets.

findings.title: No integration test covering auth retention on http→https upgrade with non-standard ports (should strip)
findings.detail: The unit tests for should_strip_auth cover the non-standard-port cases (lines 1607-1608), but there is no integration/end-to-end test that actually performs an http→https redirect with a non-standard port and verifies the Authorization header is removed in practice via rebuild_auth.

Returns a generator of Responses or Requests."""`). Add a period for consistency.
